coredata.dc:description: High salinity and alkalinity of saline-alkali soil lead to soil deterioration, the subsequent osmotic stress and ion toxicity inhibited crops growth and productivity. In this research, 8 mg kg−1 and 16 mg kg−1 functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) can alleviate the adverse effects of saline-alkali on tomato plant at both seedling and harvest stages, thanks to their up-regulation effects on soil properties and plant physiological processes. On one hand, FCNs stimulate the plant potential of tolerance to saline-alkali and disease resistance through triggering the defense response of antioxidant system, enhancing the osmotic adjustment, promoting the nutrient uptake, transportation and utilization, and up-regulating the photosynthesis, thereby improve tomato growth and productivity in saline-alkali soils. On the other hand, FCNs application contributes to the improvement of soil physicochemical properties and fertilities, as well as decline soil salinity and alkalinity, which are related to plant growth and fruit quality. This research also focuses on the dose-dependent effects of FCNs on their regulation effects and toxicity to tomato growth under stress or non-stress. These findings recommend that FCNs could be applied as potential amendments to ameliorate the saline-alkali soil and improve the tomato tolerance and productivity in the Yellow River Delta.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM image (a), FTIR spectrum (b), C1s XPS spectrum (c) and Zeta-pH curve (d) of FCNs.

Soil properties influenced by FCNs with different doses. (a): Soil pH and CEC after treatment with FCNs. (b-d): Soil AN, AP and AK after emendation with FCNs and tomato seedlings for 14 days. Data were reported as mean ± SD (n = 4). Different lowercase letters on error bars indicate the significant differences among treatments at P < 0.05.

Physiological parameters of tomato seedlings influenced by FCNs with different doses. (a): FWs of aerial part, root and total FWs of tomato seedlings under saline-alkali stress for 14 days. (b): Contents of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b and total chlorophyll in upper leaves. (c-h): MDA contents, SOD, APX, POD, GSH activities, and PRO contents in leaves and roots of tomato seedlings under saline-alkali stress for 14 days. Data were reported as mean ± SD (n = 4). Different lowercase letters on error bars indicate the significant differences among treatments at P < 0.05.

Plant heights (a), S-L FWs (b), FWs of single fruit and fruit diameters (c), BER incidences and fruit morphologies (d), as well as shoot nutrient contents (e) of tomato plants at harvest stage with different treatment in saline-alkaline or neutral soils. Data in (a-d) and (e) were taken in quadruplicate (n = 4) and triplicate (n = 3), respectively. All the data are reported as mean ± SD. Different lowercase letters on error bars indicate the significant differences among treatments at P < 0.05.

Antioxidant systems, osmolytes contents and fruit flavor substances levels in tomato plant with different treatment at harvest time in saline-alkaline or neutral soils. (a): MDA in leaves. (b-e): SOD, POD, APX, CAT activities in leaves. (f, g): GSH and PRO contents in leaves. (h): AsA contents in leaves and fruits. (i): Soluble sugar contents in leaves, roots and fruits. All the data are reported as mean ± SD (n = 4). Different lowercase letters on error bars indicate the significant differences among treatments at P < 0.05.

head.ce:title: Functional carbon nanodots improve soil quality and tomato tolerance in saline-alkali soils

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High salinity and alkalinity of saline-alkali soil lead to soil deterioration, the subsequent osmotic stress and ion toxicity inhibited crops growth and productivity. In this research, 8 mg kg−1 and 16 mg kg−1 functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) can alleviate the adverse effects of saline-alkali on tomato plant at both seedling and harvest stages, thanks to their up-regulation effects on soil properties and plant physiological processes. On one hand, FCNs stimulate the plant potential of tolerance to saline-alkali and disease resistance through triggering the defense response of antioxidant system, enhancing the osmotic adjustment, promoting the nutrient uptake, transportation and utilization, and up-regulating the photosynthesis, thereby improve tomato growth and productivity in saline-alkali soils. On the other hand, FCNs application contributes to the improvement of soil physicochemical properties and fertilities, as well as decline soil salinity and alkalinity, which are related to plant growth and fruit quality. This research also focuses on the dose-dependent effects of FCNs on their regulation effects and toxicity to tomato growth under stress or non-stress. These findings recommend that FCNs could be applied as potential amendments to ameliorate the saline-alkali soil and improve the tomato tolerance and productivity in the Yellow River Delta.

ce:section.ce:para: Soil salinization has become the main pressure restricting the quality of agricultural output and the improvement of land use efficiency (Xia et al., 2019). There are approximately 11 million hm2 of saline-alkali soil worldwide, and the total area is increasing at a rate of 10% each year (Cui et al., 2021). The Yellow River Delta in China is a typical coastal saline soil formed by the accumulation of large amounts of sediment under the influence of land and sea (Cui et al., 2021). The environmental and human factors, such as low groundwater depth, high salinity, high evaporation-precipitation ratio, poor drainage, magnify soil degradation and secondary soil salinization (Zhang et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2020). Due to the severely high mineral content, nutrient deficiencies and disordered microbial activities, there is limited vegetation coverage in the saline-alkali soil (Zhao et al., 2020). In turn, the lack of vegetation coverage seriously constrains the soil nutrient supply, soil carbon stocks and microbial community (Zheng et al., 2018; Bai et al., 2020). As an important reserve land resource for food production, the amelioration and utilization of salinized soil is in urgent need under the background of global food crisis (Zhang et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2020). However, the existing technologies (e.g., washing, phytoremediation, and organic matter additions) for amending the coastal soils have been restrained due to their high costs, low efficiency, or secondary pollution (Luo et al., 2017). Therefore, an effective, low cost and eco-friendly measure is urgently needed for improving soil properties, mitigating saline-alkali stress, and promoting plant productivity in coastal salinized soil.

Nano-technology can be effective and economical in soil remediation and plant growth regulation in saline-alkali land compared with traditional methods, opening the new situation of agricultural environment improvement (Sabet et al., 2018). Growing researches have been reported that nanoparticles show direct influence on plant physiological action or indirect effect on soil environment, thus positively regulate plants growth and tolerance, such as induce seed germination, improve plant photosynthesis, promote nutrient absorption and fertilizer utilization efficiency, upregulate antioxidant system, and boost crop productivity (Martínez-Ballesta et al., 2020; Dimkpa et al., 2019; Ahmadian et al., 2021). Carbon-based nanomaterials have many potential applications in plant biotechnology due to their unique physical and structural properties (Feng et al., 2021). The application of graphene oxide (GO) with an appropriate amount increase the roots length, the leaves area and number, the nutrients and water absorption, as well as the activities of antioxidant enzymes, which eventually enhance the resistance of plants to severe stress (Mahmoud and Abdelhameed, 2021). Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) can enter plant cells and increase their water uptake, thus alleviate the salt stress induced damage and promote broccoli growth in the saline environment (Ballesta et al., 2016). Graphene of 5 g kg−1 can improve alfalfa growth and tolerance in the saline-alkali soil by modulating various physiological, especially the photosynthesis and antioxidant system (Chen and Wang, 2021). Nanographene oxides (NGO) can alleviate the negative effects of salinity stresses via increasing phenolics biosynthesis in plants (Golkar et al., 2021). As a type of carbon based material with widely concern, biochar of sufficient amount (3% of biochar weight / soil weight in Cui's et al., 2021, 5–20 ton ha−1 in Shi et al., 2022) can be used as a good soil amendment to improve soil fertility and productivity. However, because of the complicated regulation mechanism of nanomaterials on plants and salinized soils, as well as the inconsistent effects of various nanomaterials with different surface structures and dose on various species of plants (Chen and Wang, 2021), there are limited studies on the morphological and physiological regulative mechanism of coupling effects between carbon dots (CDs) and saline-alkali in crops, as well as the regulation and phytotoxic effects of CDs on the saline-alkali soils.

The water-soluble functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) synthesized from an electrolysis method show outstanding up-regulation effects on plant growth in the whole biological cycle, as well as the soil properties and microbial communities of the arid land (Chen et al., 2021a,b; Chen et al., 2022). The direct effects of FCNs on plants are predominantly ascribed to their chemical structure and properties (Chen et al., 2020a, 2021a, 2022). On one hand, the acid and electronegative functional groups on FCNs can provide negative charges in medium to active nutrient ions and biomacromolecules functions, thus improve soil properties, nutrient availability and microbial diversity. On the other hand, the nanoscale structure and abundant hydrophilic functional groups enable FCNs acting as a transportation to deliver water and nutrient in plant organs thereby booming plant germination and growth. Besides, FCNs can also accelerate plant growth and scavenging of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by enhancing root activity, photosynthesis and antioxidant system functions, resulting in significantly increased resistance of plant under abiotic stress (Chen et al., 2020b, 2022). Based on their outstanding performance on plants and soils, FCNs have the ability to amend the quality and productivity of saline-alkali soils and mitigate their adverse effect on plant growth in saline-alkali land.

To reveal the regulation and phytotoxic effects of FCNs on the saline-alkali soil, long-term effects of FCNs on soil amelioration and crop productivity in the saline-alkali soil from the Yellow River Delta, China was investigated. Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum L.), an economically important crop grown in most regions of the world including salinized land (Yan et al., 2021), was chosen for this study. This research aimed to (1) reveal the effects of FCNs on the physicochemical properties, fertility, productivity and microbial community of saline-alkali soils; (2) ascertain the role of FCNs in improving saline-alkali soils, as well as promoting tomato growth and tolerance during the vegetative and reproductive periods; (3) identify the main influencing factors of saline-alkali soils on tomato growth, as well as the biosecurity of FCNs.

FCNs used in this study were synthesized using only graphite and water by our previous reported electrolysis method (Chen et al., 2020a, 2021a), which is described in supplementary materials (SMs). The corn straw biochar was purchased from Lize Environmental Protection Technology Co., LTD, China.

Saline-alkali soil samples were collected from the Agricultural High-tech Industry Demonstration Zone of Yellow River Delta (118.66° N, 37.32° E), Dongying, Shandong Province, China. The soil samples were randomly selected from the topsoil (0–20 cm) and grounded to pass through a 2 mm sieve. The properties of the soil were as follows: pH 8.95, Cation exchange capacity (CEC) 9.01 cmol kg−1, soil salt content 3.92 g kg−1, soil organic carbon (SOC) 19.05 g kg−1, total nitrogen (TN) 0.80 g kg−1, total phosphate (TP) 0.63 g kg−1, total sulphur (TS) 1.00 g kg−1, total potassium (TK) 16.73 g kg−1.

Tomato seeds (Ailsa Craig) were provided by Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences. Seeds of uniform size were surface sterilized with ethanol (70%) followed by thorough rinsing with distilled water. The matrix in greenhouse were supplied with half strength Hoagland solution and regularly monitored until the expansion of the fourth main leaves. The seedlings were prepared for further experiments. The condition of greenhouse was: day/night temperature 28 °C/25 °C, 16 h light/8 h dark and 50% humidity.

FCNs solution of 200 mL with different concentrations were supplemented in saline-alkali soil. The content of FCNs solution is prepared according to the ratio of dry FCNs in dry soils, including: 0 mg kg−1, 4 mg kg−1, 8 mg kg−1, 16 mg kg−1 and 32 mg kg−1. After the soils were naturally dried and loosen, the tomato seedlings were transplanted into pots and cultivated in greenhouse for two weeks. After transplanting, the seedlings were irrigated with 200 mL 1/20 Hoagland culture solution. All the pots were arranged in complete randomized block design in quadruplicate for each treatment. The growth and physiological parameters of plants, as well as the quality of soils were statistically analyzed to reveal the response of the tomato seedlings in vegetable growth stage to FCNs and determine the dosage of FCNs in saline-alkali soil remediation.

By the above experiments, 8 mg kg−1 and 16 mg kg−1 FCNs were selected for further experiments to investigate their effects on the development of tomato at harvest stage. As biochar was a kind of carbon-based materials that showed remediation effect on soil quality in the saline-alkali soil of the Yellow River Delta (Cui et al., 2021), biochar of 16 mg kg−1 was chosen as the comparison in this experiment. The tomato seedlings were transplanted in the pots filled with saline-alkali soils or neutral garden soils that pretreated with FCNs or biochar. The treatments were grouped as SCT (saline-alkali soil without FCNs), SF8 (saline-alkali soil +8 mg kg−1 FCNs), SF16 (saline-alkali soil +16 mg kg−1 FCNs), SB16 (saline-alkali soil +16 mg kg−1 biochar), NS (non-salinized soil without FCNs), NSF8 (non-salinized soil +8 mg kg−1 FCNs), NSF16 (non-salinized soil +16 mg kg−1 FCNs). All the pots were arranged in complete randomized block design with four replicates for each treatment. After 85 days of cultivation, the plant and soil samples were collected and prepared for the further determination.

The morphology, structure and Zeta potential of FCNs were characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL-1011, Japan), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker Tensor 37, Germany), Raman spectrometer (invia, Renishaw, Britain), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, America) and Malvin laser particle size analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Britain).

The rhizosphere soil sample was collected by shaking the soil off the roots in each pot. The pH of the soils before and after treatment was determined using a pH meter (soil/water, 1:5). Soil bulk density (SBD) was measured using the cutting ring method (Liang et al., 2021). TN and TC were determined using an elemental analyzer (EA III, Vario MACRO cube, German). TP and TK were tested by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, optima 7000, America) after digestion by hydrofluoric acid- nitric acid-perchloric acid (5:3:1). Available N (AN) was determined by alkali diffusion method (Bao, 2005), available phosphorus (AP) was analyzed by molybdenum antimony anti colorimetry, and available potassium (AK) was tested by NH4Ac extraction plus flame photometry (Pansu and Gautheyrou, 2006). CEC was determined by ammonium chloride-ammonium acetate exchange method. Soil salinity was assessed by the gravimetric method (soil/water, 1:5) (Yang et al., 2016). Dissolved soil organic matter (SOM) and SOC concentrations were determined with potassium dichromate by volumetric method (Osman et al., 2013). The concentrations of the soluble cations K+, Mg2+, and Na+ were measured by ICP-OES (optima 7000, America). Soil sucrase (S-SC), urease (URE), catalase (CAT), polyphenol oxidase (PPO) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity were assayed by spectrophotometry (Shao et al., 2018; Qu et al., 2020; Cui et al., 2021) using testing kits purchased from Suzhou Keming Biotechnology Co., LTD., China. The bacterial community diversity of saline-alkali soils was determined by high throughput sequencing (Cao et al., 2022).

After harvesting, the tomato plants were rinsed with flowed water to clean the surface, blotted the surface moisture, and the roots, stems, leaves, and fruits were separated. Fresh weight (FW) of the plant tissue was weighed immediately, then dry weight (DW) was measured after blanching at 105 °C for 15 min and drying to constant weight at 75 °C by oven. The plant height was measured at the early flowering stage. Blossom-end rot (BER) is a common physiological disorder under stress (Shao et al., 2018). In full productive age, five fresh fruits were randomly sampled from each replication to measure the fruit weights and diameters. The incidence of BER was counted manually. The chlorophyll (Chl) in upper leaves were extracted by 80% (v/v) acetone and measured by spectrophotometry at 663 and 645 nm using an ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (UV, Thermo Evolution 220, America) (Lichtent and Wellburn, 1983). The enzyme activities, such as peroxidase (POD), superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), ascorbate peroxidase (APX) and glutathione reductase (GR), were measured by UV (Cao et al., 2022) using the reagent kits. Malondialdehyde (MDA) content was determined in fresh leaves using thiobarbituric acid (Heath and Packer, 1968). Reduced glutathione (GSH) content was estimated with dithionitrobenzoic acid at 412 nm by UV (Ellman, 1959). Contents of soluble sugars and proline (PRO) were estimated in dry leaves samples by UV (Eliaspour et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2020). The ascorbic acid (AsA) content was tested with fast blue salt B at 420 nm by UV (Zhang and Huang, 1993). P, K, Mg, Ca and Na contents in dry leaves were determined by ICP-OES after digesting by sulfuric. N and S contents were analyzed by organic element analyzer.
Details of the determination process are described in SMs.

Statistical analyses were performed with the software package SPSS 19.0 (IBM, Armonk, New York, USA). The data were presented as an arithmetic means ± standard deviations (SDs). Differences between treatments were statistically analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). According to LSD Tests, the significant differences (P < 0.05) were labeled with different lowercase letters in all figures and tables. A Pearson correlation was conducted to analyze correlations between plant parameters and soil quality.

FCNs used in this research are carbon nanodots with size of 4–15 nm as shown in TEM and HRTEM photos (Fig. 1a, Fig. S1). FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1b, detailed information in Table S1) prove that plenty of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl (1712 cm−1, 1456 cm−1), ester (1712 cm−1, 1248 cm−1), hydroxyl (3000 cm−1, 1361 cm−1, 610 cm−1), carbonyl, ether (1162 cm−1) and aromatic skeleton (866 cm−1), are connected on the surface and at the edges of FCNs. XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S2) shows that the main elements in FCNs are carbon and oxygen with ratio of 1.2: 1.0. The main carbon bonds in FCNs are CC, CO and CO as shown in Fig. 1c. The high oxygen content and abundant polar functional groups endow FCNs with high hydrophilic and acidity, thus the pH of 1.0 g L−1 FCNs solution is about 2.0. Moreover, FCNs show high electronegativity due to the high content of electronegative groups. The Zeta potential of FCNs decrease from −25.0 mV to −28.8 mV with increased pH from 7.0 to 10.0 in Fig. 1(d), which also proves the good stability of FCNs under alkaline conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dosage effects of FCNs on saline-alkali soils and tomato seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: FCNs emendation can significantly decrease the soil pH and improve the soil CEC in saline-alkali soils (Fig. 2a). With addition of 4 mg kg−1 FCNs, the soil pH declines by 19.6%, while the CEC increases by 42.2%. By maximally, the soil pH declines to 7.2 (by 29.4%) and the CEC increases by 43.2%, with 32 mg kg−1 and 8 mg kg−1 of FCNs application, respectively. The nutrients availabilities of the saline-alkali soils are also improved with FCNs addition (Fig. 2b-d). 8 mg kg−1 FCNs improve AN and AP content to 1.35 and 2.15 times, respectively. However, AK content changes slightly with maximum improvement of 0.97% by 16 mg kg−1 FCNs.

After remediation by FCNs in saline-alkali soils, tomato seedlings growth is promoted, performing as the significantly increased FWs of seedlings and Chl contents in leaves (Fig. 3a, b). Specially, 16 mg kg−1 FCNs maximally improve the FW of aerial part by 35.71%, root FW by 124.52% and total FW by 50.28%, as well as improve the content of Chl a, Chl b and total Chl by 3.27 times, 3.47 times and 3.29 times, respectively. FCNs can also induce response of antioxidant system to mitigate cell oxidative damage under saline-alkali stress. From Fig. 3c, the membrane oxidative damage is mitigated according to the decreased MDA contents in both leaves and roots. Specially, FCNs of 8 mg kg−1 decrease MDA content in leaves to the greatest extent. The enzyme activities of antioxidant enzymes (include SOD, APX, POD) and levels of antioxidants (such as GSH and PRO) are significantly up-regulated by the FCNs addition (Fig. 3). The enzymes activities and antioxidants levels both in leaves and in roots are improved with the increasing FCNs dosage from 0 to 8 mg kg−1. However, activities of some enzymes (SOD, POD in leaves and roots, and APX in leaves), as well as the contents of GSH and PRO in leaves and roots decrease when the FCNs dosage is further increased.

As FCNs addition of 8 and 16 mg kg−1 show better improvement effects on the growth and antioxidation of tomato seedlings to cope with saline-alkali stress, further experiments to investigate the effects of FCNs on tomato development and saline-alkali soil remediation were conducted with these two dosages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of FCNs on plant physiological process, fruit quality and nutrient uptake at the harvest stage
ce:section.ce:para: The significant effects of FCNs on tomato heights, stem and leaf FWs (S-L FW), fruit weights and sizes are shown in Fig. 4 (P < 0.05). 8 mg kg−1 and 16 mg kg−1 FCNs addition increase the plant height by 17.02% - 20.09% and 5.77% - 8.55%, and increase the S-L FW by 40.66% - 45.81% and 36.91% - 41.92%, compared to those of SCT and SB16 groups under saline-alkali stress, respectively (Fig. 4a, b). Compared with SCT and SB16 groups, the treatment of 16 mg kg−1 of FCNs maximumly improve the weight of single fruit by 102.53% and 88.84%, as well as the fruit diameter with 21.04% and 11.46%, respectively (Fig. 4c). The significant differences are also found in S-L FW and fruit diameter of tomato plant in different groups under non-stress, revealing the universal positive effect of FCNs on plant growth and fruit development. At the fruit development stage, FCNs reduce the incidence of BER of tomato fruit in saline-alkali soils, with the maximum degree of 86.57% in SF16 group (Fig. 4d). Moreover, the disorder severity of BER is also relieved with FCNs addition, which could be judged by the size of rotting area around the fruit umbilicus (Fig. 4d).

16 mg kg−1 FCNs addition significantly increase the contents of shoot nutrients and salt ions, including N, S, P, K, Mg, Ca and Na, whereas 8 mg kg−1 FCNs only increase the contents of N, P, Mg and Ca (Fig. 4e). In comparation with SCT, 16 mg kg−1 FCNs show greater promotion effects on S (63.93%), P (25.69%), K (63.99%), Mg (43.02%), Ca (95,63%) and Na (106.73%) contents in shoot, while N content of SF8 is improved (26.81%) more significantly than that of SF16. However, the contents of K and Na in shoots are decreased by 8 mg kg−1 FCNs. In general, the promotion effect of 16 mg kg−1 FCNs on nutrient uptake efficiency of tomato plant is superior to 8 mg kg−1 FCNs or biochar.

In saline-alkali soils, the response of antioxidant systems of tomato plant treated with FCNs at harvest stage is in consistent with that in seedling stage in addition to the POD activity (Fig. 5 ). FCNs significantly mitigate the increased MDA content under saline-alkali stress, due to their stimulation effect on antioxidase (SOD, APX and CAT) activity and antioxidant compounds (GSH, PRO and AsA) levels. PRO and soluble sugar are important osmolytes in cells. The contents of PRO and soluble sugar in leaves are improved by FCNs. However, the soluble sugar content in root is decreased with FCNs, and the differences of PRO content in root are insignificant among treatments under stress. FCNs can also influence the antioxidant system of tomato plant under non-stress condition. Compared with NS group, although the activities of POD and APX decline, the activities of SOD and CAT, and the contents of AsA and soluble sugar are all improved in NSF8 and NSF16 groups. FCNs promote the AsA and soluble sugar levels in tomato fruit to improve the fruit quality and flavor both under saline-alkali stress and non-stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of FCNs on soil properties at harvest stage of tomato
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table 1 , FCNs significantly influence the properties, fertility and soluble cations of saline-alkali soils at harvest stage (P < 0.05). FCNs of 16 mg kg−1 reduce soil pH, SBD, and salinity by 5.05%, 2.14% and 22.75% compared with SCT, whereas biochar has a minimal impact on these properties. In comparison with SCT, the CEC, SOM and SOC contents are raised maximally by 54.37%, 89.22% and 88.03% with the FCNs addition, which are also more than that of biochar. Soil TC, TN, AN and AK increase by 49.63%, 70.97%, 77.13% and 16.11% with FCNs addition. However, TP, TK and AP contents are decreased by FCNs compared with SCT, which are improved by biochar. FCNs of 8 mg kg−1 increase soluble K+ content by 27.62%, and Mg2+ by 88.87%. 16 mg kg−1 FCNs also significantly decline the content of Na+ in soils by 46.67% and 43.86% than that of SCT and SB16 group, respectively. By comparison, biochar increases the content of soluble K+, but slightly decreases the contents of Mg2+ and Na+.

ALP, URE and S-SC act specific roles in the P, N and C cycles of saline soils, and are important indexes to evaluate soil fertility (Yan et al., 2021). CAT degrades H2O2 to reduce their oxidative stress on living cells, and indicates the soil oxidation intensity (Cao et al., 2022). PPO plays a key role on aromatic compounds cycling and SOC decomposition (Qu et al., 2020). There are significant up-regulation effects of FCNs on soil URE, S-SC, ALP, PPO and CAT activities (Table 2 ). Compared with SCT and SB16, FCNs at 8 and 16 mg kg−1 increase the soil URE activity by 84.39%–91.04%, S-SC activity by 26.76%–126.98%, ALP activity by 4.78%–30.67%, PPO activity by 11.00%–34.10%, and CAT activity by 1.68%–39.34%. However, in neutral soils (NS), FCNs improve URE activity, decrease ALP and CAT activities, but have no significant influence on the activities of S-SC and PPO (Table 2).

Significant correlations were found among plant growth parameters, plant physiological parameters, soil quality and soil enzymes (Table S3). The Pearson correlations of plant parameters and soil parameters demonstrate that the main factors affecting plant growth and productivity are soil pH, salinity, CEC, SOM, AP, Na+ content, URE activity and SC activity in soils (Details are in SMs).

By high throughput sequencing, the number of bacterial sequences in SF8 is the highest among the three treatments of saline-alkali soils (Table S2). Based on the results of Chao 1 index, Shannon index and relative abundance of bacterial diversity from the phylum to the species level, high dosage of FCNs may have adverse effects on soil microorganism. Fortunately, appropriate amount of FCNs emendation (8 mg kg−1 in this research) can increase the bacterial community diversity in saline-alkali soils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs promote the growth, development, tolerance and nutrient uptake of tomato
ce:section.ce:para: Excess saline-alkali stress results in osmotic stress and later causes ion toxicity, which destroys water homeostasis and normal nutrient balance of plant, thus inhibits plant photosynthesis and growth, decreases osmolytes levels, induces severe membrane peroxidation damage and physiological disorder (Jain and Guruprasad, 1989). Ions aggregation promotes the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which cause severe damages in growth, photosynthesis, antioxidant system, nutrient assimilation, gene expression, etc. (Singh et al., 2020) Besides, soil salinity and alkalinity of saline-alkali soil lead to nutrient deficiency, resulting in great reduction of plant productivity (Zhao et al., 2020). Fortunately, FCNs addition is favorable for alleviating the adverse effects of saline-alkali stress on tomato seedlings according to their up-regulation effects on plant physiological process. Regulation effects of CDs on plants have been described as inducing plants growth, development, photosynthesis, antioxidant reaction, carbohydrates synthesis, and gene expression in lower dosage (Wang et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019). Previous studies have proved the universal positive effects of FCNs on seeds germination, plant growth, tolerance and productivity, which are mainly ascribed to the up-regulation effects on plant physiological processes including antioxidant system, osmotic adjustment, gene expression and photosynthesis (Chen et al., 2021b; Chen et al., 2022). The positive effects of FCNs application on plant growth and tolerance under saline-alkali stress could be attributed to a variety of mechanisms involving material characteristics, plants physiology, and soils properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs up-regulate the antioxidant system of tomato plant
ce:section.ce:para: Saline-alkali stress disturbs the cellular redox homeostasis and induces oxidative stress, then disturbs the balance between generation and removal of ROS (Feng et al., 2021). Excess ROS can inactivate biomolecules (e.g. membrane protein, enzyme, DNA), thereby damage membrane function and permeability, cause organelle dysfunction, inhibit antioxidant system, as well as influence gene expression in plants (Rezayian et al., 2019). In mitigating oxidative damage, plants develop complex defensive mechanisms, including the non-enzymatic and enzymatic antioxidants to scavenge excessive ROS in cells (Wang et al., 2016). SOD constitutes the first step of defense mechanism against ROS toxicity, and catalyzes the conversion of the superoxide radicals (O2•−) to oxygen (O2) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Mittler, 2002). Then the toxic H2O2 is detoxified by conversion to innocuous H2O in further reactions by POD, CAT and APX (Feng et al., 2021). GSH is a non-enzymatic redox compound that eliminates O2•− and H2O2 to protect cells against lipid peroxidation (Candan and Tarhan, 2003). AsA suppresses H2O2 through ascorbate-glutathione cycle (Rezayian et al., 2019). Therefore, the efficient running of these detoxification systems is necessary to cope with the oxidative damage caused by stress (Feng et al., 2021).

Nanomaterials can effectively improve antioxidant enzyme activities, reflecting in the reduction of ROS accumulation and lipid peroxidation, and the protective effects on photosynthesis, key metabolic pathways, gene expression and growth (Sharma et al., 2019). Our results show that appropriate concentration (especially from 4 mg kg−1 to 16 mg kg−1) of FCNs treatment can significantly increase SOD, CAT, POD and APX activities, as well as GSH, PRO and AsA contents in plants (Figs. 3 and 5), thus reduce membrane lipid peroxidation, and maintain cell membrane permeability and integrity under saline-alkali stress (Li et al., 2021). FCNs-based materials show advantages in activating antioxidant enzymes both in vitro and in vivo (Chen et al., 2020b, 2022). First, CDs were demonstrated as scavengers of intracellular ROS (DPPH•, O2 •−, and •OH) to effectively alleviate the oxidative damage of plants (Li et al., 2021). Second, FCNs can immobilize with enzyme molecular to protect the structural stability of enzyme, as well as donate more active sites and negative charges to enhance the enzymatic antioxidant reaction under stress (Chen et al., 2020b). Third, FCNs can stimulate the synthesis of the antioxidant enzymes by inducing the genes expression related to antioxidant enzymes synthesis (Chen et al., 2021a). Although FCNs enhance the activities of the major antioxidant enzymes except POD in leaves at the harvest stage under saline-alkali stress, only SOD and CAT activities are improved by FCNs in neutral soils. The result is speculated due to the fact that POD and APX are more sensitive to pH or salinity. Meanwhile, the MDA content in leaves is slightly increased by 16 mg kg−1 FCNs treatment in neutral soils (Fig. 5), probably due to the FCNs-induced nano-stress. These indicate that FCNs induce different antioxidation mechanism under different culture conditions. Therefore, appropriate amount of FCNs can trigger the moderate defense response of antioxidant system, which is beneficial for crops to cope with the saline-alkali stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs facilitate the nutrient uptake and delivery in tomato plant
ce:section.ce:para: FCNs can regulate the nutrient uptake and delivery in plant to mitigate the stress caused by unbalanced nutrient structure (Chen et al., 2021a,b), thus promote plant growth and tolerance under saline-alkali stress. Although saline-alkali decreases the concentrations of microelements in shoots (Nampei et al., 2021), FCNs at 16 mg kg−1 significantly increase nutrient elements (including N, P, K, S, Mg, Ca) and Na contents in tomato shoots (Fig. 4). The result could be attributed to many factors. Firstly, FCNs protect the uptake function of the root system through alleviating the saline-alkali induced oxidative stress on root, thus enhance the absorption and utilization of nutrient elements and ions by plants (Chen and Wang, 2021). Secondly, FCNs can facilitate absorption and transportation of water, nutrients and ions via improving root activity, inducing the genes expression related to ions transport, as well as accumulating and transporting cargoes into plant via transport carrier proteins or ion channels (Chen et al., 2022). The large surface area of FCNs makes it easier for plant roots to uptake more fertilizers and thus decrease nutrient losses and promote growth (Zhang et al., 2015), which can in turn improve the roots vigor and enzymatic activity (Shojaei et al., 2019). Thirdly, based on the aromatic structure and rich electronegative functional groups, FCNs increase the availability of nutrient in soils via cation exchange, surface interactions and water availability processes (Zhao et al., 2020). The increased plant nutrient uptake may have been related to the increased soil fertility and plant nutrient availability (Cui et al., 2021), which are induced by FCNs. Nevertheless, FCN may have stronger absorbability for divalent ions in comparison with K+ and Na+, which lead to the decrease of Na and K in plants with 8 mg kg−1 FCNs application, compared to the SCT and SF16 groups.

The presence of sufficient nutrient elements is beneficial to the metabolism and growth of tomato seedlings. N, P, K, S and Mg can strengthen the function of antioxidant system (such as antioxidant enzymes and GSH), up-regulate photosynthesis (chlorophyll synthesis and CO2 exchange) and enhance osmolyte synthesis to promote the ROS scavenging system, organics synthesis and tissue water content (Ahanger et al., 2021; Ahanger et al., 2019; Dimkpa et al., 2019). The increased uptake of P and K with FCNs application also lead to the improvement of reproductive growth and yield for tomato. Reasonable amounts of K and Ca are required to maintain the structural and functional integrity of plant membranes under salt stress (Zhu, 2016). Ca also plays a crucial role in cell wall stabilization and regulation of ion transport (Zhu, 2016). In addition, higher N, P and K uptake by plants implied that FCNs can induce a positive priming effect on soil nutrient cycling (Agegnehu et al., 2017). Due to the improved absorption and transport ability of cations in plant, FCNs also increase sodium content in shoot. Although the Na content in plants is also increased by 16 mg kg−1 FCNs, the increased antioxidant system can eliminate the adverse effects of ion stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs enhance the osmotic adjustment in tomato plant
ce:section.ce:para: According to the increased ions and osmolytes contents in tissue, FCNs show up-regulation effects on osmotic adjustment of tomato plant in both seedling and fruit development period (Figs. 3 and 5). The enhanced nutrient uptake promotes the synthesis of osmotic substances, such as PRO and water-soluble sugar, which can increase the osmotic and aqueous potential to help plants tolerate osmotic stress (Zhao et al., 2020). Osmolytes can preserve optimal cell water potential and defend DNA and macromolecule structures, thus regulate physiobiochemical pathways in plants, including maintaining membrane integrity, sustaining optimal redox state, and regulating salt-stress-responsive gene expression (Farouk et al., 2020). In addition, PRO can also induce ROS reduction, as well as soluble sugar can provide energy and carbon backbones needed for cell development and secondary metabolite production (Alamri et al., 2018). FCNs increase the PRO and soluble sugar contents in tissue, indicating that FCNs improve nutrients uptake and transportation by tomato in saline-alkali soils. Therefore, the increased osmolytes that mediated by FCNs may contribute to better growth, development and tolerance of tomato in saline-alkali soils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs regulate the growth, development and tolerance of tomato
ce:section.ce:para: ROS induced by saline-alkali stress could reduce chlorophyll biosynthesis and damage the ultrastructure and function of chloroplast, thus reduce the photosynthetic activity in plant leaves (Farouk et al., 2020). Salt stress also damages light-capturing pigment complexes on photosystem I and photosystem II (LHC I and LHC II), reducing light absorption, electron transfer by chloroplast, and energy conversion effects, which ultimately results in the reduced photosynthetic efficiency (Mu and Chen, 2020). Fortunately, FCNs were proved to be able to accelerate cell division and nutrient accumulation (e.g. Mg, N) in leaves, thus improve leaf thickness and photosynthetic pigment synthesis to enhance photosynthesis (Chen et al., 2021a). Besides, increased activity of antioxidant enzymes aroused by FCNs addition can quickly eliminate saline-alkali triggering ROS, thus improve membrane stability to prevent the decline in photosynthesis (Feng et al., 2021). Moreover, CDs can not only enhance leaves to optimize the light usage and accelerate the electron transport, but also improve the rubisco activity and PSII functioning, thereby contributing to increased productivity of leaf photosynthesis (Sai et al., 2018, Khatri and Rathore, 2018, Swift et al., 2020). Therefore, FCNs increase the plant biomass and sugar contents due to their up-regulation effect on photosynthesis process.

Sugars and organic acids are the major components of fruit soluble solids that influence fruits flavor (Yan et al., 2021). FCNs improve the contents of soluble sugar and AsA, as well as the fruit diameter and weight, thus improve fruit flavor and quality (Figs. 4 and 5). Moreover, FCNs decline the accidence of BER of tomato fruit in saline-alkali soils (Fig. 4), further improve the plant productivity. As one of the common physiological disorders in tomato growing on saline-alkali soils, BER reduces fruit yield and quality, resulting in economic losses in tomato production (Shao et al., 2018). FCNs exhibit significant effects on reduction of soil salinity, enhancement of ALP activity (Tables 1 and 2) and increase of the relative abundance of Proteobacteria and Acidobacteria in soils (Chen et al., 2022), thus reduce the prevalence and severity of BER within tomato fruit (Shao et al., 2018).

Based on the above, moderate amount of FCNs show significantly positive effects on tomato plant growth, tolerance and nutrient uptake under saline-alkali stress, which are more efficient than biochar addition. This is owing to that the up-regulation effects of 16 mg kg−1 biochar on plant physiology and soil properties were weaker than FCNs at the same concentration. However, there are dose-dependent effects of FCNs on regulating plant growth, antioxidant system and osmotic adjustment at seedling stage of tomato under saline-alkali stress. The excessive FCNs application suppress plant growth and stress tolerance (Fig. 2), probably due to the osmotic stress and oxidative stress caused by high concentration of FCNs, which is similar to the effect of some other carbon nanomaterials on plants (Cruces et al., 2021; Fang et al., 2022; Jiang et al., 2021). Besides, excessive amount of FCNs could accumulate more salt and nutrient, thus form aggregates in the soils and inhibit the nutrient uptake by plants. Fortunately, there is no nanostructured substances remaining in soils and plant tissues (Fig. S3), suggesting that FCNs is safe to the soil environment and food chain.

Therefore, FCNs amendment can significantly moderate the negative effect of saline-alkali on tomato growth and development at both seedling and harvest stage, which are due to their participation in enhancing the enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidant systems, regulating the osmotic adjustment, activating photosynthesis, improving the membrane structure stability and promoting water and nutrient uptake, thereby stimulating the potential of plant tolerance to environmental stress and disease.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs improve soil property, fertility and enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: Salinity-alkali induces various types of soil degradations, such as compaction, inhibited aeration, nutrient deficiency, and reduction of microbial activities and functions (Saifullah et al., 2018). Extreme soil pH can directly affect plant root function, alter nutrient availability and disturb the balance of ions and mineral nutrition (Peng et al., 2008). The saline-alkali induced reduction of soil quality obviously inhibits tomato growth and development. Fortunately, FCNs addition significantly improves the property (e.g. pH, CEC, salinity, SOC, SBD) and fertility (e.g. SOM, TN, TK, TP, AN, AK, AP, K+, Mg2+) of saline-alkali soils (Table 1), thus results in the enhancement of plant growth and fruit quality.

The present results show the increase in CEC, SOC and nutrients contents, while decrease in pH, salinity, SBD and Na+ concentration in rhizosphere soils at the seedling and harvest stage after the FCNs application. Due to the abundant acidic functional groups, including carboxyl and hydroxyl, FCNs supply negative charges and H+, neutralize alkali ions and active cation in saline-alkali soils to notably reduce the soil alkalinity, improve CEC and enhance soil buffer capacity. Owing to the excellent cationic adsorption and desorption properties of FCNs, the saline ions move with water to the subsoil after irrigation, which accelerate the leaching of salt and reduce the topsoil salinity (Yan et al., 2021). In addition, the reduced SBD reveals that FCNs can increase soil porosity, which is resulted from the enhanced root development and vigor improving soil structure and creating water channels (Saifullah et al., 2018). In turn, the increased soil porosity and soil hydraulic conductivity promote water movement in soil, thus contribute to the absorption of nutrients and water by roots, as well as accelerate the leaching of salts (Cui et al., 2021). Then the empty soil exchange sites caused by Na+ leaching could be replaced by nutrient cations, which causes higher nutrient concentration in soils (Zhao et al., 2020). The increased soil CEC by FCNs could be explained by the rise of water-soluble ions (e.g. K+, Na+, and Mg2+) in soils (Yan et al., 2021). Because of the abundant organic groups, FCNs have an affinity for organic molecules, and cause their aggregation and polymerization to form organic matter in soils. SOM can not only contribute to the increase of CEC, but also show a certain buffer effect based on its high adsorption capacity, thus neutralize soil alkalinity, delay the return of salt to soil and improve soil nutrients (Yuan et al., 2019). The changes in soil physical and chemical properties may promote microbial activities and plant growth, and in turn increase the input of exogenous carbon as well as the formation of endogenous carbon (Cui et al., 2021), eventually resulting in the increased SOC.

Increased AN with FCNs addition is partly attributed to the acceleration of soil N transformations and enhancement of N mineralization induced by the increased microbial and enzyme activities (Ameloot et al., 2015). In addition, the increase of plant root exudates and soil enzymes contents also lead to the increase of soil TN. The content of AP in soil decreases at harvest stage, which is because the potassium in soil is easily lossy with irrigation, and a large amount of AP is uptake and utilized by plants with the growth and develop of plants (Zhao et al., 2020). AN, AP and AK contents increase obviously after FCN treatment at seedling stage, however, TP, TK and AP decrease by FCNs during the tomato harvest. This fact is attributed to that FCNs promote tomato to enter exuberant reproductive period earlier, which induce plant to consume more P and K (Zhao et al., 2020).

Overall, the application of FCNs improves the soil nutrient structure, including SOM, available nutrients and total N, P, K, Mg (Fig. 2 and Table 1). Unlike biochar that can serve as additional source of nutrients (e.g. SOM, N, P and K), FCNs elevate soil fertility by enhancement of CEC and soil enzymes (e.g. ALP, URE, S-SC, PPO and CAT in Table 2). Moreover, FCNs can preserve nutrients in soils due to their high absorption capacity to nutrient and soils. Soil enzyme sources comprise secretions of soil animals, plants and microorganisms (Shao et al., 2018). Enzyme activity is not only one of the indicators of soil quality and soil fertility (Alkorta et al., 2003), but also plays a role in promoting benign ecological cycles in soil and preventing pests and diseases (Badiane et al., 2001).

The activities of soil enzymes are affected by plant rhizosphere secretions, and also by soil property (pH, salinity, SOM, etc.) and microbial activity (Qu et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). The extreme pH and salinity destroy the molecular structure of enzyme and seriously inhibit enzyme activity. The declined activities of ALP in NSF8 and NSF16 are owing to the reduced soil pH with FCNs application. The enhancement of SOM increases the activity of antioxidant enzymes, as well as makes favorable environment for microorganisms (Haider et al., 2020). In turn, the rich microbial communities result in greater accumulation of carbon in soil through various metabolic activities of microbial communities, thereby positively affect nitrogen fixing and organic matter decomposition (Talaat, 2019; Haider et al., 2020). FCNs can upregulate microbial communities in soils (Table S2), thus stimulate microbial and enzymatic activity to accelerate the SOM decomposition, promote nutrient availability and improve soil fertility (Chen et al., 2022). For example, FCNs can promote the microbial community that beneficial to plant in saline-alkali soils, such as Proteobacteria and Acidobacteria (Chen et al., 2022), which have significant positive correlations with phosphatase activity (Shao et al., 2018).

These results indicate that FCNs can regulate soil property and microbial communities, thereby accelerate soil enzyme activity, promote nutrient availability and improve soil fertility, ultimately resulting in stimulating nutrient acquisition, improving plant growth and yield. The developed root system in turn increase soil porosity, enhancing enzyme activity and improving soil fertility. Therefore, FCNs induce a virtuous cycle between soil properties, microbial community, enzyme activity, fertility and plant growth in saline-alkali soils. FCNs can be used as a conditioner to boost up soil carbon sequestration potential and enhance soil quality on alkaline soil.

In summary, FCNs improve the growth, tolerance and fruit quality of tomato, and minimize the negative effects of saline-alkali stress on plant in saline-alkali soils, which is mainly through their direct effect of regulating plant physiology processes and indirect effect of improving soil properties and fertility. Application of FCNs up-regulate the physiological processes of plant, including chlorophyll formation, enzymatic activation, osmolyte synthesis, and nutrient uptake, then mitigates the reduction of dry matter and fruit quality aroused by saline-alkali stress. Besides, FCNs improve soil properties like pH, SBD, salinity, fertility and soil enzyme activity, which also benefit for tomato growth to counteract the saline-alkali mediated damage. Generally, these findings help us better understanding how FCNs affect the growth and development of tomato under saline-alkali stress, and improve property and fertility of saline-alkali soil. The positive effects of FCNs on soil property and fruit quality are crucial for developing more sustainable practices in agricultural activity in Yellow River Delta.
